title: Pea and ham soup
subtitle: Split peas simmered with a ham hock until rich and thick
excerpt: Yellow split peas and a whole ham hock simmered for two hours produce a thick, deeply savoury soup. Pull the ham into shreds and stir them back in. One of the best cold-weather bowls there is.
sourceNotes:
Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861) ,  Project Gutenberg #10136. Pea soup with ham or bacon is one of the oldest British soup recipes, appearing in Beeton as both a plain and a cream version.

Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845) ,  Project Gutenberg. Green pea and split pea soup methods.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool completely and freeze in portions for up to three months. The soup thickens considerably on freezing; thin with a little water or stock when reheating.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Improves over two days in the fridge as the split peas continue to thicken. Thin with hot water or stock when reheating.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dried pea that has had its outer skin removed and been split in half. Unlike whole dried peas, split peas dissolve into a thick purée without soaking or blending.

content.text: Pea and ham soup is built on the same principle as a good stock: a whole ham hock simmers with split peas until the peas dissolve, the meat falls apart, and the liquid turns thick and savoury. It takes two hours but almost no attention. The result is the kind of soup that makes a cold day feel manageable.
dissolve more readily than green split peas, giving a smoother, more unified soup. Both work; yellow produces the golden colour associated with this dish.
Place the {{ham-hock}} ham hock in a large stockpot. Cover with 2 litres of cold water. Bring to the boil, then discard this first water (it removes excess salt and impurities).

Refill the pot with 2 litres of cold water. Add the {{split-peas-yellow}} of split peas, {{onion}} onion, {{carrot}} carrots, {{celery}} celery, {{bay-leaves}} of bay, and {{thyme}} of thyme. Bring to a simmer.
Simmer gently for 90 minutes to 2 hours, skimming any foam from the top in the first 20 minutes. The peas should dissolve almost entirely, and the hock should be falling off the bone.
Lift the hock out of the pot. Remove and discard the bay leaves, thyme stalks, and the halved onion. Pull the meat from the bone and shred into pieces. Discard the skin, bone, and any gristle.
Use a stick blender to partially blend the soup, leaving some texture from the carrot and celery. Or blend completely for a smoother result.
Return the shredded ham to the soup. Taste: the hock provides a lot of salt, so add cautiously. Thin with a little hot water if the soup is very thick.
Ladle into deep bowls and scatter flat-leaf parsley over the top. Crusty bread alongside.

items.cause: The hock was very salty, or it was not blanched in the first water.
items.fix: Always discard the first water. If still too salty after cooking, add a peeled potato to the pot and simmer for 20 minutes: the potato absorbs some salt. Then remove and discard it.

items.symptom: Split peas have not dissolved after two hours
items.cause: The peas were old, or the simmer was too gentle.
items.fix: Old split peas take longer and may never dissolve completely. Buy from a shop with high turnover. If the peas are not fully dissolved but the soup is otherwise ready, blend it fully with the stick blender.
Ham hocks vary in size. If the meat still resists after 2 hours, continue simmering. The hock is ready when the meat pulls apart easily with two forks.

items.symptom: Soup is very thick the next day
items.cause: Split pea soups always thicken as they cool and rest.
items.fix: This is expected. Add hot water or stock until it reaches the right consistency and reheat gently.

content.text: Pea and ham soup is one of the oldest soup recipes in the British canon. The combination of dried peas and salted pork appears in Elizabethan cookery books and has remained in continuous domestic use ever since. The split pea itself is a practical ingredient: no soaking, no blending required, it simply dissolves into a thick, satisfying base over time. Victorian household cookbooks listed it as a cheap, nourishing meal for a large household, and that remains its primary job today.
